speaker: interviewee
text: It is an interesting episode, because I-- as far as religion goes and, as I said earlier before, I was actually far from it. The only episode that was-- that I had with religion was that of my grandmother, my father's mother, which I loved very much. And she insisted as much as she could to send me and-- and teach me the Hebrew alphabet and reading Hebrew when I was about five years or so. And that was my only memory and contact with religion. Because I really was never in a synagogue and never went to prayers and nothing. But that was my only contact with religion. And I would say maybe more my memory of my grandmother than religion that brought me actually when I had the choice to choose between being brought up secular or religious that I took that religion way. And I choose to go into a religion-- to a religion place, which-- and as I mentioned, I went from one place. And I didn't think it's religious enough, and I organized a group of eight children, and we went to another place. We went to Jerusalem from Tel Aviv, Bnei Brak, and we went to Jerusalem. And that was very, very religious, very, very orthodox. And I was actually what you could see traditional Jewish, Eastern European religious with the black dress and the payes, which I have pictures of them. And all this stuff, with all this stuff. And I was in a yeshiva. But it all ended when I was 15 years old. By that time, 14, 15, between 14 and 15-- '47, so I was 14-- and it started-- I started to-- to doubt the whole thing, the whole concept about religion, except-- especially the very meaning of very orthodox religion. And when my mother came and in early '48, in February '48, I left the yeshiva in Jerusalem. And Jerusalem was already under siege, because it already started the war of independence. Jerusalem was under siege. And I had left Jerusalem with the last transport that managed to leave Jerusalem before Jerusalem was completely sealed off. And I came to Tel Aviv where my mother was. And I went to that kibbutz where I met my wife. And I met her there, and since there we have-- since then, we are together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from early 1948. February-- actually 27th of February, 1948 I came to that kibbutz, and I met my wife. And since then, we are together. So that is actually my history of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Palestine. Afterward-- after we left the kibbutz, I was in that kibbutz for about eight months. And then our group left as a-- as a group to another kibbutz. And they formed a kibbutz group. And we--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife and me left, and we went to Tel Aviv. We stayed in Tel Aviv. We both went to work at the age of 15, and we both continued to study high school in the evening. We was finished. And-- and at the age of 20, we got married. Her aunt used to say, oh, those two, they were like twins. They were born together. And she used to always to [INAUDIBLE], she was very-- she liked to talk a lot and she used to count, like when we met we were 14. She was like, oh, they both together are half of my age. They are both together are only 28 years old. Both together. She used, oh, they reached already 30 together, you know, so-- so we grew up together. And we stayed together, and we had two children and four grandchildren. We left Israel in '67. It was-- there were a few reasons for it. And among the most important was that my mother-in-law, which was actually my mother's aunt, but we used to call her mother, and my children used to call her grandmother. They didn't know till we were-- till years after she had passed away, and we were in Canada that she wasn't actually her real mother. She passed away half a year before the Six Day War. I was always in the army. I was with the tanks. And it was a small army. And since tanks battalions weren't that many, so I was always in the army. And with the fact that my mother-in-law passed away, my wife couldn't take it anymore. She says, I can't. She had a nervous breakdown. I mean, not really severe but bad enough. And I did very well in Israel. Actually better than I really am doing now today here. So financially, I-- I didn't lack anything in Israel. But because of the experience of the war, I figured, OK. Maybe-- maybe my wife is right. She was the one that insisted really to leave. And because of her, I figured OK. So I sold the business, and I sold the house, and I sold everything, and we came here. And we started at everything from new. And thank God for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I did. I think I-- unless you want to know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message for your children or grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't have a message-- one message is not to forget that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who we are and where we come from. Our ancestors paid a very heavy price for it. So I think we honor to them to remember. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, Ariela. [? Leventhal ?] Rojek. That's my daughter, Orly. Those are her two daughters, Hilete and Shira Stein. That's my daughter, Avital, the younger one. And that's Hailey, her daughter. And that's Maya, the youngest one. The biggest mouse. That's my father, Saul. He is 17 years old in that picture. That was taken in 1927 in Warsaw. He gave that picture to a friend of his that left Warsaw for the United States. And many years later, we learned about the existence of that picture. And we managed to get it. His friend was living in New York. And they managed to get it and make a copy of it. That is my first picture taken in Israel, in Palestine, in 1943, March-- around March 1943. And it was taken in [INAUDIBLE] Hadera. That's an agriculture school in Hadera. I am the fourth child from the left. And in the first row down, below the third child from the left is my brother, Arieh. That's the second picture that I know that exists that I took in Palestine. That was also taken in 1943. I guess-- I don't know exactly the month, but in 1943. And that was taken in Bnei Brak, where I was already-- the place that I was assigned afterwards, after Hadera. On the left is a friend of mine, [INAUDIBLE] his name. In the middle am I, and on the left is my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right, sorry. On the right is my brother Arieh. That's my wife's and my wedding picture, which was taken in Tel Aviv in 1953. That's the picture of my mother and me. It was taken in Tel Aviv in 1993.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Sarah Rojek. That's the picture of my brother and me. It was taken-- it was taken in September of the 1994 in Albany, New York at my cousin's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's you, which one's your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother is on my left, and I am on the right. That's my brother's children. They live in Tel Aviv, Israel. On the left is his daughter, [? Michal. ?] And in the middle is the youngest one, [? Dori. ?] And on the right is his older son and that's [INAUDIBLE]. They both are married. The two older, they are married with children.
